title: Handling Images & Diagrams
concept: Generating captions or descriptions for images so their content becomes searchable text.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, how did we preserve the structure of tables for document search?structure-aware table extraction, how do we best preserve a table's structure for search?

options.text: Strip out all formatting and merge all cells into a single continuous sentence.
Convert tables into structured markdown or a row-per-chunk format to keep relationships.
Convert every table cell into a separate, independent search document.

exercises.explain: Converting tables to structured formats like Markdown preserves the rows, columns, and relationships between data points so that semantic search engines can query them effectively.

cards.title: The Blind Spot in Search
cards.body: Documents are full of diagrams, charts, and photos. But standard search engines and LLMs are blind to raw pixel data, meaning valuable visual information gets completely ignored during a search.

cards.title: Image Description Generation
cards.body: Generating descriptions for images involves using a vision-language model to translate visual information—like diagrams, charts, and photos—into descriptive text. This text is then stored and indexed, making the visual data searchable by traditional text search engines.

cards.title: The Treasure Chest Key
cards.body: Think of an image inside your document like a locked treasure chest. An image-to-text model acts like a key, unlocking the chest to describe exactly what is inside so that search engines can easily index its contents.

cards.title: Unlocking a Water Cycle Diagram
cards.body: If a PDF page contains a diagram of a water cycle, we pass it to a vision model which outputs: 'A diagram showing evaporation, condensation, and precipitation.' Now, a search for 'condensation' will successfully find this page!

cards.title: OCR Isn't Enough
cards.body: Many believe that standard OCR (Optical Character Recognition) is enough for images. While OCR reads printed words, it completely misses the meaning of layout, shapes, arrows, and visual relationships in a diagram.

cards.title: Raw Pixels vs. Described Pixels
cards.body: Compare how search engines see images when we leave them as raw pixels versus when we generate text descriptions.

left.text: Search engines only see 'chart.png' and ignore the actual content.

right.text: Search engines read: 'A bar chart showing a 15% increase in user retention over 6 months.'

exercises.prompt: What is the most effective tool to turn a complex flow chart into searchable content for RAG?

options.text: A simple text scraper that extracts metadata like filename and size.
A basic OCR tool that only reads individual words from left to right.
A vision-language model that generates a descriptive text summary of the visual.
An image compression algorithm that reduces file size.

exercises.explain: Vision-language models can understand visual context, labels, and relationships to write an accurate, detailed text summary of what is happening in the image.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Modern vision models can describe the relationships and flow of steps in a diagram, not just list the text labels.
exercises.explain: Vision-Language Models can interpret the meaning of shapes, lines, and relationships in a diagram, translating them into descriptive paragraphs.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of why we describe diagrams:
exercises.sentence: We convert visual details into searchable text so that search engines can find them.
exercises.explain: Converting visual details into searchable text is the core process that allows search engines to find image content.

exercises.prompt: Which of these descriptions of a chart was generated by an AI description assistant?

options.text: Figure 4.2 shows a bar chart. Please refer to page 12 for the actual numbers.
Upload the flowchart.png to the cloud storage bucket under the assets folder.
This image is a bar chart titled 'Quarterly Revenue' showing sales growth from Q1 to Q4, with Q4 showing the highest peak at fifty thousand dollars.

exercises.explain: Option C is clearly written by an AI assistant attempting to describe a visual layout in a structured, conversational manner suitable for indexing. Options A and B are simple manual notes or instructions.

exercises.prompt: You are building a RAG system for a textbook containing complex physics diagrams. How should you process these diagrams so they are searchable alongside the book's text?

options.text: Delete the image completely since LLMs cannot search raw pixels anyway.
Store the raw binary image data inside the vector database as a text string.
Generate a descriptive caption of the diagram and append it to that page's text chunk.
Convert the diagram into a table of pixel coordinates and color values.

exercises.explain: The best approach is to generate a descriptive text caption of the image and append it to the page's chunk. This ensures text search queries can match words inside the diagram without losing the context of the page it appeared on.
